one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning an LLM to read node text plus GNN posterior probabilities yields a stronger, dimension-agnostic link-stealing attack -- >90% white-box and ~80-90% black-box accuracy on citation graphs, with one model transferring across datasets -- but the approach hinges on the strong assumption that the attacker already holds each node's textual attributes.
key_finding: White-box (Table III, LLM vs. best prior MLP attack of He et al. [11]): the LLM attack reaches >=90% accuracy/F1 on all four datasets -- Cora 90.34% acc, Citeseer 93.26%, Pubmed 94.08%, Ogbn-arxiv 97.48% -- improving Pubmed by ~8% over the best prior method and Cora/Citeseer by ~3%. Black-box (Table VI): LLM gets Cora 86.76%, Citeseer 90.49%, Pubmed 79.40%, Ogbn-arxiv 88.25% accuracy, beating the best prior black-box baseline by ~9% on Ogbn-arxiv, though it slightly trails PP+Feature(Max) on Pubmed (79.40 vs 84.89). A single LLM generalizes across datasets: trained on Ogbn-arxiv and attacking Cora it hits 89.93% accuracy, exceeding the 87.89% of the best prior attack trained directly on Cora. Multi-dataset fine-tuning further beats single-dataset training, especially on the data-scarce Cora/Citeseer. Attack succeeds across all three attacker LLMs and all three target GNNs (>90% white-box); GAT target and Vicuna-13B attacker are strongest in white-box, but in black-box the mid-size Vicuna-7B beats Vicuna-13B because fine-tuning uses only the shadow task.
core_contribution: The paper (Guan et al.) proposes using fine-tuned Large Language Models as the attack model for LINK STEALING attacks against Graph Neural Networks. The authors' own contribution has two parts: (1) reframing the link-inference attack as a text-generation/classification task so that an LLM can jointly consume a node pair's TEXTUAL features (paper titles + abstracts) together with the numerical posterior probabilities returned by the target GNN, rather than relying on posterior probabilities alone as prior link-stealing work did; and (2) because LLMs handle variable-length input, a SINGLE fine-tuned LLM can perform link stealing across datasets with different numbers of classes (different posterior-probability dimensions), which fixed-dimension MLP attackers could not do. They design two prompt templates: a white-box prompt that asks 'do they have a link?' (fine-tuned on known links) and a black-box prompt that instead asks the shadow-task question 'do they belong to the same category?' (since same-class nodes are more likely linked). They claim to be the first to use LLMs for this attack. NOTE: LLM-on-graph works cited (He et al. [15], Ye et al. [36], Liu et al. [16], GraphEdit [24]) are Related Work, not this paper's contribution -- those enhance benign GNN tasks; the novelty here is turning the LLM into a privacy-attack tool.
threat_model: Attacker wants to infer whether an edge (link) exists between two nodes in the private training graph of a deployed GNN node-classification service, a privacy violation (edges can be sensitive: social ties, citation structure). Attacker has black-box query access to the target GNN and obtains posterior probability vectors per queried node. Two knowledge levels are studied: WHITE-BOX -- attacker additionally knows the link status of some node pairs (assumed 2,000 known links for Cora/Citeseer, 5,000 for Pubmed, 30,000 for Ogbn-arxiv) and uses them to fine-tune directly on the link task; BLACK-BOX -- attacker knows NO links and no model internals, only node features, and fine-tunes on the same-class shadow task instead. In both settings the attacker is assumed to possess the nodes' textual attributes (titles/abstracts), which is a strong and load-bearing assumption. Attacker does not know GNN architecture or parameters.
limitations: 1) All four datasets are citation graphs with rich text attributes; the method fundamentally depends on nodes having meaningful textual features, so it is untested on and likely inapplicable to non-text-attributed graphs (social/molecular/transaction graphs). 2) The attack ASSUMES the adversary already possesses each node's title+abstract -- a strong prerequisite the paper does not justify obtaining. 3) The 'theoretical analysis' is hand-wavy: the subset relation X1 subset of X2 and the postulated gain coefficient alpha>1 are assumptions, not proofs. 4) Black-box cross-dataset transfer degrades notably (e.g. attacking Pubmed from other datasets falls to ~54-72% because Pubmed has only 3 classes / low-information posteriors), so the single-model generalization claim is weaker in black-box. 5) White-box requires thousands of known ground-truth links (2k-30k), a heavy knowledge assumption. 6) No defenses (e.g. output perturbation, DP) are evaluated, so real-world robustness of the attack is unknown. 7) LLM fine-tuning is far more compute-costly than the MLP baselines, a trade-off not discussed.

datasets_used: Cora (2,708 nodes, 1,433 feats, 10,556 links, 7 classes; 2,000 known links assumed in white-box)
Citeseer (3,327 nodes, 3,703 feats, 9,228 links, 6 classes; 2,000 known links)
Pubmed (19,717 nodes, 500 feats, 88,651 links, 3 classes; 5,000 known links)
Ogbn-arxiv (169,343 nodes, 128 feats, 1,166,243 links, 40 classes; 30,000 known links)
quantitative_results: White-box LLM accuracy (Table III): Cora 90.34%, Citeseer 93.26%, Pubmed 94.08%, Ogbn-arxiv 97.48% (F1 essentially identical)
White-box improvement over best prior method: ~8% on Pubmed, ~3% on Cora/Citeseer
Black-box LLM accuracy (Table VI): Cora 86.76%, Citeseer 90.49%, Pubmed 79.40%, Ogbn-arxiv 88.25%
Black-box: ~9% accuracy gain over best prior baseline on Ogbn-arxiv; slightly trails PP+Feature(Max) on Pubmed (79.40 vs 84.89)
Cross-dataset transfer: LLM trained on Ogbn-arxiv attacking Cora = 89.93% acc, beating best prior model trained directly on Cora (87.89%)
Attacker-LLM white-box (Table IV): Vicuna-13B best, >92% acc/F1 all datasets (e.g. Ogbn-arxiv 97.76%); Vicuna-7B and LongChat both >90%
Target-GNN white-box (Table V): all >90%; GAT strongest (Cora 93.46%, Citeseer 95.87%)
Black-box attacker-LLM (Table VIII): Vicuna-7B (e.g. Citeseer 91.75%) outperforms Vicuna-13B on the shadow-task fine-tuning; LongChat weakest
Prior black-box instability: PP+Feature shows a 34% Cosine-vs-Euclidean gap on Pubmed; Max-vs-Mean similarity metrics differ by ~23 points (Table VII)
baselines_compared: Feature [11] (He et al. USENIX 2021, node-feature MLP)
Eight similarity metrics from He et al. [11] reported as Mean and Max (Cosine, Euclidean, Correlation, Chebyshev, Braycurtis, Canberra, Cityblock, Sqeuclidean)
LINKTELLER [14] (Wu et al. SSP 2022, influence-analysis edge recovery)
